medications.drug_name: topical corticosteroid (e.g. betamethasone valerate scalp application)
medications.indication_rationale: Mid-potency topical corticosteroid is a reasonable first-line option for limited alopecia areata but is not urgent and is best initiated by the GP after examination; many focal patches regrow spontaneously.
Systemic steroids are not indicated for mild focal alopecia areata; the risk/benefit does not favour them for limited disease.
No evidence of tinea capitis (smooth non-scaling skin, no lymphadenopathy); empirical antifungals are not indicated.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Limit scalp-checking to once daily after showering rather than repeatedly through the day.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces hypervigilance and health anxiety, which are the dominant distressing features.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like alopecia areata — a common, non-infectious, non-scarring condition where the immune system temporarily rests some hair follicles. It is not cancer and not contagious.
Hair often regrows on its own over weeks to months in a small single patch. No emergency treatment is needed.
See your regular GP within about a week for an examination and some screening blood tests (thyroid and iron).

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Confirm diagnosis on examination, review thyroid/iron screening, assess for any new patches or systemic features.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Rapidly spreading or multiple new patches, any scalp scaling/redness, or systemic symptoms (fever, rash, joint pain).

must_include_items: Reassurance that this is a benign, non-infectious, non-scarring condition
Screen for tinea (scaling/lymphadenopathy) and systemic autoimmune features before reassuring
Routine (non-urgent) GP follow-up within ~1 week with thyroid/iron screening
acceptable_alternatives: Offering mid-potency topical corticosteroid via the GP
Watchful waiting given high spontaneous regrowth rate
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for infectious tinea or systemic autoimmune features
No safety-netting for spreading patches or systemic symptoms
errors_of_commission: Escalating to the emergency department or urgent care for a painless localised patch in a well patient
Prescribing empirical antifungals or systemic steroids
Ordering a scalp biopsy for a classic focal patch
Amplifying the patient's health anxiety with alarming language
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a low-acuity (T1) case. Appropriate reassurance with danger-mimic screening and routine follow-up passes; emergency escalation is an over-triage failure.
